function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

    22|
    23|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    24|

    27|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    28|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    29|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    30|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    31|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    36|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    37|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    38|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    39|
    40|## Agent Guidance
    41|
    42|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    43|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    44|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    45|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    46|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    47|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other

    16|
    17|# Case Phase Transitions
    18|
    19|> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Observed phase transitions across cases:
    24|
    25|### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
    26|- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
    27|- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
    28|- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
    29|- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact
    30|
    31|### File Setup -> Treatment
    32|- **Trigger**: All providers confirmed, initial records/bills requests sent
    33|- **Prerequisites**: Insurance claims set up, LOR sent, medical providers identified
    34|- **Actions on transition**: Begin treatment monitoring, send records requests
    35|- **Timeline**: Days after file setup
    36|
    37|### Treatment -> Demand in Progress
    38|- **Trigger**: Client confirms treatment has concluded, or attorney directs
    39|- **Prerequisites**: Treatment complete, preferably all records received
    40|- **Actions on transition**: Confirm treatment status with client, request outstanding records/bills, begin demand preparation
    41|- **Timeline**: Weeks to months after treatment ends (waiting for records)
    42|
    43|### Demand in Progress -> Negotiation
    44|- **Trigger**: Demand package sent to adjuster
    45|- **Prerequisites**: All records/bills received, demand drafted and approved
    46|- **Note**: In transfer cases, may skip directly to Negotiation
    47|
    48|## Evidence
    49|
    50|- In Case A, the case went directly from Lead to File Setup to Negotiation (skipping Treatment and Demand) because the prior firm had already handled treatment and sent a demand.
    51|- In Case B, the transition from Treatment to Demand in Progress was triggered by the attorney noting "no activity since August" and directing staff to check in with the client. Once treatment was confirmed complete, the phase change occurred.
    52|- In Case C, the case went through Lead -> File Setup -> Treatment sequentially, with clear phase change entries in the activity log.
    53|
    54|## Agent Guidance
    55|
    56|1. Track phase change prerequisites as a checklist
    57|2. Do not advance a phase until all prerequisites are met
    58|3. Log every phase change with the person who authorized it and the date
    59|4. When an attorney asks about a case, report the current phase and what's blocking the next transition
    60|5. For transfer cases, evaluate which phases were already completed by the prior firm
    61|6. The attorney should approve all phase transitions from Demand in Progress onward
    62|
    63|## Pitfalls
    64|
    65|- Advancing to Negotiation without a demand in the file (observed in Case A)
    66|- Not confirming treatment completion with the client before transitioning to Demand
    67|- Letting cases sit in Treatment for months without checking on client status
    68|- Not logging the reason for the phase change
    69|
    70|## Related

    73|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — timing of the phase transition to Demand matters
    74|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — certain transitions require attorney review

    16|
    17|# Pre-Settlement Funding Obligations
    18|
    19|> Some PI clients obtain "pre-settlement funding" (lawsuit loans) from third-party lenders while their case is pending. These loans create obligations that must be satisfied from the settlement proceeds, reducing the client's net recovery. The firm must understand its obligation to the funder and factor the loan into settlement math.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Pre-settlement funding creates a complex financial picture at settlement:
    24|
    25|1. Client takes a loan from a funding company (e.g., "Claims Angel") during the pendency of the case
    26|2. The loan accrues interest and fees over time
    27|3. At settlement, the funding must be repaid from the client's share of proceeds
    28|4. The attorney must factor the loan payoff into the disbursement sheet
    29|
    30|Settlement math with pre-settlement funding:
    31|- Gross settlement
    32|- Less: Attorney fees
    33|- Less: Case costs
    34|- Less: Medical provider payments/liens
    35|- Less: Pre-settlement funding payoff
    36|- Equals: Client's net recovery
    37|
    38|When the settlement offer is modest, pre-settlement funding can consume a large portion of what's left, leaving the client with very little.
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case A, the client had "loans" from a company named "Claims Angel" totaling $6,537.05. With an $18K offer, after paying the loan ($6,537.05) and 50% of the medical provider bill ($5,397.50), and attorney fees, the client would receive less than $3K. The paralegal noted: "I'm not sure what obligation we have to satisfy the presettlement funding."
    43|
    44|## Agent Guidance
    45|
    46|1. During intake, ask if the client has obtained any pre-settlement funding or lawsuit loans
    47|2. If yes, request the funding agreement and current payoff amount
    48|3. Log the funder name, original loan amount, and current payoff in the case financial records
    49|4. Factor the funding payoff into all settlement evaluations
    50|5. When presenting settlement options to the client, show the complete disbursement breakdown including the funding payoff
    51|6. Ask the attorney about the firm's legal obligation to satisfy the funding (varies by jurisdiction and agreement)
    52|7. If the settlement barely covers obligations, flag this to the attorney early
    53|
    54|## Pitfalls
    55|
    56|- Not discovering the pre-settlement funding until settlement disbursement
    57|- Not understanding the firm's legal obligation to the funder
    58|- Not factoring funding into the settlement evaluation, leading to unrealistic client expectations
    59|- Assuming the payoff amount is the same as the original loan (interest and fees accrue)
    60|

    64|- [[demand-value-factors]] — knowing the funding obligation influences demand strategy
    65|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — liens and funding together determine net recovery

    16|
    17|# Health Insurance Lien Management
    18|
    19|> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|The health insurance lien lifecycle:
    24|
    25|1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
    26|2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
    27|3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
    28|4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
    29|5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger
    30|6. **Settlement Integration**: Factor the adjusted lien into the settlement math
    31|7. **Negotiation**: After settlement, negotiate a reduction of the lien (typically firms negotiate a percentage reduction)
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case C, the health insurer (Anthem) asserted a subrogation lien that included a 5/16/2024 date of service for an angiogram. The attorney identified that this procedure was scheduled prior to the accident (on 4/10/2024) for a pre-existing cardiac condition and was unrelated to the MVA. The paralegal emailed the lien handler (Carelon) requesting removal of the unrelated dates of service. An updated lien ledger with the charges removed was received the same day.
    36|
    37|## Agent Guidance
    38|
    39|1. When a subrogation notice arrives, log it immediately and request a detailed itemization
    40|2. Compare every date of service on the lien against the client's accident-related treatment dates
    41|3. Flag any dates of service that fall outside the treatment window or relate to unrelated conditions
    42|4. Draft a dispute letter to the lien handler citing the specific dates and reasons for removal
    43|5. Obtain pre-accident medical records if needed to prove a condition was pre-existing
    44|6. Keep the updated lien ledger in the case file for settlement calculations
    45|7. Alert the attorney whenever the lien amount changes materially
    46|
    47|## Pitfalls
    48|
    49|- Accepting the lien amount at face value without auditing it
    50|- Not knowing the client's pre-existing medical history (makes it impossible to identify unrelated charges)
    51|- Delaying the lien dispute — the longer you wait, the harder it is to resolve before settlement
    52|- Confusing the health insurance lien with PIP payments (they are separate obligations)
    53|- Not getting the updated lien ledger in writing after charges are removed
    54|
    55|## Related
    56|
    57|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments reduce what health insurance pays (and thus the lien)
    58|- [[demand-value-factors]] — the lien amount affects net recovery and settlement strategy
    59|- [[pre-existing-condition-separation]] — separating accident-related from pre-existing treatment

    19|
    20|## Pattern
    21|
    22|SOL monitoring follows a countdown pattern with escalation triggers:
    23|
    24|- **180 days out**: Initial alert — ensure case is actively being worked
    25|- **90 days out**: Elevated alert — settlement or suit filing decision must be imminent
    26|- **30 days out**: Critical alert — attorney must decide: file suit, accept offer, or take other action
    27|

    36|
    37|## Agent Guidance
    38|
    39|1. Calculate the SOL date at intake and enter it in the case management system

    16|
    17|# Attorney Review Triggers
    18|
    19|> Certain events in a PI case require the attorney's direct involvement and approval. These are decision points where paralegal-level handling is insufficient and attorney judgment is needed. Identifying and routing these triggers efficiently prevents bottlenecks and ensures quality control.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Attorney review is triggered by:
    24|
    25|1. **Initial Case Evaluation**: When a transfer case file arrives, the attorney reviews the prior firm's work, demand status, and financial picture before directing next steps
    26|2. **Stale Case Flag**: When the attorney notices no activity on a case for an extended period (2+ months), they direct staff to check in with the client
    27|3. **Lien Disputes**: When unrelated charges appear on a health insurance lien, the attorney directs which items to dispute
    28|4. **Settlement Authority**: Client gives authority to "negotiate to LBF" (Last Best and Final) — the attorney must approve the negotiation range
    29|5. **Case Complexity Flags**: Pre-settlement funding obligations, minimal treatment, incarcerated clients — these require attorney guidance on strategy
    30|6. **Demand Approval**: Before a demand is sent, the attorney reviews and approves
    31|7. **File Completion Escalation**: When records collection stalls, the attorney escalates to ensure action
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case A, the attorney reviewed the transfer case and identified key issues: prior demand already sent, treatment gap, pre-settlement funding, and minimal treatment. He then directed staff on next steps.
    36|- In Case B, the attorney flagged the case noting "no activity since the middle of August" and directed a check-in with the client. Later, the attorney changed the phase from Treatment to Demand in Progress.
    37|- In Case C, the attorney identified that unrelated cardiac treatment was included in the Anthem lien and directed its removal. He also escalated when records requests had "no action" for a month.
    38|
    39|## Agent Guidance
    40|
    41|1. Route the following to the attorney immediately:
    42|   - New transfer case files received (with summary memo)
    43|   - Settlement offers received
    44|   - Lien itemizations with potential disputes
    45|   - Client requests for case status update (if case has been inactive 30+ days)
    46|   - Bills going to collections
    47|   - Pre-settlement funding documentation
    48|2. When routing to the attorney, include a brief summary and recommended action
    49|3. Track attorney-pending items and follow up if no response within 48 hours
    50|4. Document the attorney's direction in the activity log verbatim
    51|
    52|## Pitfalls
    53|
    54|- Not routing complex issues to the attorney (trying to handle them at paralegal level)
    55|- Routing too many routine items to the attorney (creating bottlenecks)
    56|- Not including sufficient context when routing (attorney has to dig through the file)
    57|- Not following up on items pending attorney review
    58|- Not documenting the attorney's direction
    59|
    60|## Related
    61|
    62|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — phase changes from Demand onward require attorney approval
    63|- [[settlement-offer-evaluation]] — offers always require attorney evaluation
    64|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — lien disputes require attorney direction

# Multi-Carrier Multi-Defendant Case Management

> When an accident involves multiple at-fault parties (e.g., chain-reaction collisions), the plaintiff's firm must manage separate BI claims against each defendant's carrier simultaneously. Each carrier may have different adjusters, different liability postures, different policy limits, and different negotiation timelines. Coordination across these parallel tracks is critical to maximize recovery.

## Pattern

Multi-carrier cases follow a parallel-track structure:

1. **Liability Determination per Defendant**: Each carrier independently evaluates their insured's liability percentage. One carrier may accept liability quickly while another delays.
2. **Separate LOR/Claim Setup**: A Letter of Representation and claim must be established with each carrier independently.
3. **Independent Negotiation Tracks**: Each carrier negotiates independently. One carrier may make an early offer while another has not engaged. Litigation may be filed against one defendant while negotiating with another.
4. **Adjuster Turnover Risk**: With more carriers come more adjusters, and turnover is more likely over the extended timeline these cases require.
5. **Common Medical Records**: All carriers receive the same medical records/bills, but each may evaluate them differently based on their insured's alleged liability share.

## Evidence

- In Case D (3-vehicle chain-reaction MVA), the firm managed separate BI claims against West Bend Mutual Insurance, Progressive Insurance Company, and American Transit Insurance, plus PIP claims against Farmers Insurance and American Transit. West Bend accepted 100% liability within 2 weeks, while the other carriers moved at different speeds. West Bend's first offer (~$13K) was rejected, leading to litigation. Meanwhile, Progressive's adjuster initiated contact for settlement discussions approximately 2.5 years later. By late 2025, the adjuster from Progressive was repeatedly calling and leaving voicemails to discuss settlement — while the West Bend litigation was in active discovery with IMEs and depositions. The case also involved a 3rd-party vehicle claim with USAA (no demand). Each carrier required separate tracking.

## Agent Guidance

1. Create a separate insurance claim record for each carrier during file setup
2. Track each carrier's negotiation status independently (offer history, adjuster contacts, last communication)
3. File a single set of LORs but maintain separate follow-up schedules for each carrier
4. When litigation is filed against one defendant, continue negotiating with other carriers
5. Track adjuster changes — request the new adjuster's contact info promptly when reassignment occurs
6. Ensure the attorney has a consolidated view of all carriers' positions for strategic decisions
7. Keep a matrix of which carriers have been sent records, which have made offers, and which are in litigation

## Pitfalls

- Treating all carriers as a single negotiation (they have independent liability analyses)
- Letting one carrier's intransigence stall progress with the others
- Not tracking which adjusters are currently assigned to each claim (turnover is common)
- Failing to notice when one carrier's adjuster is proactively reaching out while attention is focused on litigation with another
- Not considering the total recovery across all carriers when evaluating individual offers

## Related

- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP claims run as an additional parallel track
- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — liens must be allocated across settlements from multiple carriers
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — a case can be in litigation against one defendant and negotiation with another simultaneously
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — multi-carrier strategy decisions require attorney involvement
"}

# Concurrent Accident Injury Apportionment

> When a client is involved in multiple accidents within a short time frame, each defendant's insurer will argue that the other accident caused the injuries. Apportionment — determining which accident caused which injuries — becomes a central issue that affects medical records organization, demand preparation, and negotiation strategy.

## Pattern

Concurrent accident cases create several challenges:

1. **Overlapping Treatment**: The client may see the same providers for injuries from both accidents, making it difficult to attribute specific treatment to a specific accident.
2. **Each Carrier Blames the Other**: Each BI carrier will argue the other accident caused the client's injuries, reducing their own liability.
3. **Separate Case Files**: The firm typically opens separate case files for each accident, but the medical history overlaps significantly.
4. **Provider Confusion**: Providers need clear guidance on which accident to attribute treatment to, as their records and billing will be used as evidence.
5. **IME Complications**: Defense-ordered Independent Medical Examinations in one case will inevitably address injuries from the other accident.

## Evidence

- In Case D, the client was involved in a chain-reaction MVA on 11/08/2022 and then a second MVA on 12/09/2022 (exactly one month later). Both cases were handled by the same firm. The second accident involved a NYC cab (American Transit Insurance). The client reported "concussion" injuries from the first accident and "neck & back" from the second. The firm set up separate case files but cross-referenced them in the activity logs ("Prior MVA 11/08/2022" and "New MVA 12/10/2022" notes). Some providers (e.g., Innovation Open MRI in Florida) were seeing the client for injuries from both accidents, and the firm had to determine which claim each provider visit should be attributed to. The attorney needed to manage apportionment strategy across both cases simultaneously. A court order in the first case required 2 defense IMEs, which would inevitably address injuries from both incidents.

## Agent Guidance

1. Open separate case files for each accident but cross-reference them prominently
2. During provider setup, note which accident each provider visit relates to
3. When requesting records, specify the date-of-accident range to avoid getting mixed records
4. Coordinate records requests for both cases simultaneously to ensure consistency
5. Flag any provider who treated the client for both accidents — their records will need careful review
6. Brief the attorney on the apportionment issue early, as it affects demand strategy for both cases
7. When an IME is ordered in one case, prepare the client for questions about the other accident
8. Track billings separately by accident to support clean demand preparation

## Pitfalls

- Mixing up which bills belong to which accident (undermines demand credibility)
- Not discovering the second accident during intake (the client may not mention it)
- Allowing defense to use the second accident to diminish recovery on the first
- Not coordinating strategy across both cases (settling one cheaply can affect the other)
- Failing to obtain records from providers who treated for the "other" accident to support your causation argument

## Related

- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — a second accident can be used as a treatment gap defense for the first
- [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] — concurrent accidents multiply the number of carriers
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — apportionment strategy requires attorney judgment
- [[medical-records-collection]] — records must be carefully attributed to the correct accident
"}

# Adjuster Persistent Unreturned Calls Pattern

> Insurance adjusters sometimes repeatedly call the firm, leaving multiple voicemails over weeks or months without getting a return call. This pattern can occur on both sides: adjusters trying to settle may be unable to reach the attorney, or the firm may deliberately deprioritize a carrier's calls while focused on other aspects of the case. Both directions carry risk.

## Pattern

Two distinct patterns emerge:

### Adjuster-Initiated Persistence
When an adjuster repeatedly calls and leaves voicemails "to discuss settlement," this may indicate:
1. The adjuster has settlement authority and wants to close the file
2. The carrier is under pressure to resolve the claim
3. The adjuster's repeated calls create a documented record of their attempts

### Firm Non-Response
When the firm does not return adjuster calls promptly, this may be because:
1. The attorney is focused on litigation with a different carrier for the same case
2. The case is not ready for negotiation on that particular claim
3. The calls are being logged as "missed calls" without a follow-up task being created
4. Staff turnover means no one is monitoring that specific carrier's communications

## Evidence

- In Case D, the Progressive Insurance adjuster called the attorney repeatedly over a 3-month period (August through October 2025), leaving 7+ voicemails. Each voicemail was transcribed and logged as a "missed call" in Filevine, but no return calls were documented during this period. The voicemails progressively indicated the adjuster wanted "to discuss settlement" and "to talk this one over." Meanwhile, the firm was actively engaged in litigation against a different defendant (West Bend) on the same case. Eventually, the attorney did engage in email correspondence with this adjuster about the claim. The pattern suggests that when a firm is managing multiple parallel tracks, some carriers' outreach can fall through the cracks.

## Agent Guidance

1. Log every adjuster voicemail with the transcription and create a follow-up task
2. Even if the case is not ready for negotiation on that claim, return calls within 48 hours with a brief status update
3. Track the number of unreturned adjuster calls — more than 3 should trigger an alert to the attorney
4. When an adjuster says they want to "discuss settlement," this is a priority callback
5. Not responding to adjuster outreach can result in the adjuster closing the file or reducing their authority
6. If the firm is deliberately delaying engagement with a carrier for strategic reasons, document the attorney's direction to do so

## Pitfalls

- Letting adjuster calls go unreturned for weeks (damages the relationship and may reduce settlement authority)
- Not creating follow-up tasks from missed call logs (the pattern just accumulates without action)
- Assuming the adjuster will keep calling forever (they may close the file or reassign)
- Missing the signal that the adjuster is ready to settle while the firm is focused on litigation elsewhere
- Not distinguishing between strategic non-response (attorney-directed) and operational failure (nobody noticed)

## Related

- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — persistent adjuster calls should be escalated to the attorney
- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — adjuster may cite non-communication as a factor
- [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] — multiple carriers means more calls to track
"}

# Filing Litigation as Negotiation Leverage

> This connection documents how [[case-phase-transitions]] from Negotiation to Litigation is not simply a failure of negotiation but a deliberate strategic choice that interacts with [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]], [[attorney-review-triggers]], and [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] to create leverage for better outcomes.

## Connection

Three concepts converge when litigation is used as leverage:

1. **Low Initial Offer** ([[attorney-review-triggers]]): The BI carrier makes an initial offer that values only a fraction of the treatment (e.g., considering only the ER visit while ignoring extensive follow-up care). The attorney evaluates the offer and determines it is inadequate.

2. **Pre-emptive Filing** ([[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] + [[case-phase-transitions]]): Rather than continuing to negotiate from a weak position, the attorney files suit well before the SOL deadline. This signals to the carrier that the firm is serious and the case will not be resolved cheaply.

3. **Parallel Negotiation** ([[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]]): When multiple carriers are involved, filing suit against one defendant can create leverage with the others. The non-litigating carriers see active litigation and may become more motivated to settle to avoid being drawn in.

## Evidence

- In Case D, West Bend's first BI offer was ~$13K, considering only the initial ER visit despite the client having 20+ providers and extensive treatment for concussion, spine, and cervical injuries. The attorney filed suit in Jefferson Circuit Court approximately 1 year post-accident. The litigation then drove discovery, depositions, and defense IMEs over the next 2 years. Meanwhile, the Progressive adjuster (a different carrier on the same case) began proactively calling to discuss settlement. The adjuster noted in a voicemail that they wanted to "talk this one over" — suggesting the existence of active litigation against a co-defendant may have motivated engagement.

## Insight

Filing suit is not a last resort — it is a strategic tool. In cases with multiple defendants, it can unlock movement on otherwise stalled negotiations. The cost of litigation (court filing fees, court reporters, preparation time) is an investment in leverage. Cases where the initial offer is dramatically below the treatment value are strong candidates for this strategy.

## Related

- [[case-phase-transitions]] — litigation is a phase transition, not just a negotiation failure
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — the decision to file requires attorney judgment
- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — filing preserves the claim regardless of negotiation outcome
- [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] — litigation against one creates pressure on others
"}
